name: Lacto Sweet Corn
subtitle: Savory, funky, sweet kernels
blrNote: Use fresh cobs from the market, not frozen kernels. The wild yeast is on the cob.

images.caption: Lacto Sweet Corn
images.alt: Lacto Sweet Corn - Savory, funky, sweet kernels

tldr: Corn kernels in brine. They get a "cheesy," funky depth that is incredible in salads.

steps.title: Cut
steps.instruction: Slice the kernels off the cob.
tips: Stand the cob upright in a large bowl and slice downward - the bowl catches flying kernels.
After cutting kernels, scrape the cob with the back of the knife to extract the starchy 'milk' - it feeds fermentation.
Use the freshest corn possible; sugars convert to lactic acid faster when the corn hasn't been stored long.

steps.title: Jar
steps.instruction: Pack into a jar and cover with brine. Corn floats, so use a weight!
tips: A zip-lock bag filled with brine makes an excellent weight that also seals out air.
Leave at least 1 inch of headspace - corn ferments vigorously and can overflow.
If using a narrow-mouth jar, a cabbage leaf trimmed to fit works perfectly as a natural weight holder.

steps.title: Ferment
steps.instruction: 3–5 days. It will smell a bit like corn chips (masa). That's good.
tips: Taste daily starting day 3 - corn ferments fast in warm climates and can become too sour quickly.
The brine will turn cloudy and slightly yellow; this is normal and indicates active fermentation.
Once it reaches your preferred sourness, move to the fridge where it will keep for 2-3 months.

culturalContext.story: Inspired by 'Chicha,' the ancient South American corn ferment with roots stretching back over 5,000 years to the Inca Empire. While Chicha is traditionally chewed and fermented into a ceremonial drink, this recipe keeps the corn as a solid food, preserving the funky, cheesy depth that lacto-fermentation brings to fresh sweet corn kernels.
culturalContext.historicalNote: Nixtamalization - treating corn with alkaline solutions - was developed by Mesoamerican peoples thousands of years ago to unlock nutrients. Lacto-fermentation achieves a similar nutritional benefit through microbial action, making B vitamins and minerals more bioavailable without the lye treatment.
culturalContext.significance: Corn fermentation represents one of humanity's oldest food preservation techniques, practiced independently across the Americas, Africa, and Asia. It bridges indigenous knowledge with modern probiotic science.
culturalContext.funFact: Fermenting corn makes the Niacin (Vitamin B3) more available to your body - the same reason tortillas made from nixtamalized corn prevented pellagra in Mesoamerican populations.

variations.name: Elote-Style Fermented Corn
variations.description: Add chili flakes, a squeeze of lime juice, and a pinch of cumin to the brine. After fermenting, serve with cotija cheese and mayo for a fermented take on Mexican street corn.

variations.name: Smoky Chipotle Corn
variations.description: Add 1-2 dried chipotle peppers to the jar before fermenting. The smoke flavor infuses the kernels over the fermentation period, creating a deeply savory condiment for grain bowls.

variations.name: Corn and Pepper Relish
variations.description: Add diced bell peppers, a sliced jalapeño, and a clove of garlic to the jar. Ferment together for a ready-made relish that works on hot dogs, burgers, or tacos.

thingsToAccountFor.title: Corn Floats Aggressively
thingsToAccountFor.description: Corn kernels are less dense than brine and will float to the surface, exposing them to air and risking mold. Always use a weight or a brine-filled zip-lock bag to keep every kernel submerged.

thingsToAccountFor.title: Vigorous Fermentation
thingsToAccountFor.description: Corn sugars ferment rapidly, producing a lot of CO2 in the first 48 hours. Burp your jar at least twice daily or use an airlock lid to prevent pressure buildup and potential overflow.

thingsToAccountFor.title: Frozen Corn Won't Work Well
thingsToAccountFor.description: Frozen corn has been blanched, killing the natural lactobacillus on the surface. If you must use frozen, add a tablespoon of raw sauerkraut brine as a starter culture.

dehydratorIntegration.method: Drain the fermented corn thoroughly and spread in a single layer on dehydrator trays lined with mesh sheets. Dehydrate until kernels are completely hard and brittle.
dehydratorIntegration.result: Crunchy, tangy corn bits - like fermented corn nuts. Excellent as a salad topper, snack, or ground into a funky corn powder for seasoning.
tips: Toss with a light coating of oil and chili powder before dehydrating for a spicy snack version.
Dehydrated fermented corn can be ground in a spice grinder to make a probiotic-rich seasoning powder.
